old_claim.note: Construction provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because classical construction practice is not a prerequisite for the scoped projectile-motion and kinematics claim.

source_shape.source_locator: Renn and Valleriani separate Galileo's first new science, strength of materials, from the second new science of accelerated motion in the Third and Fourth Days; the sampled node is scoped to projectile motion and kinematics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports Galileo's projectile-motion scope through accelerated motion and artillery practice, while treating construction-related strength of materials as a different part of the Discorsi.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This justifies removing construction from the direct prerequisite set because the node is not scoped to Galileo's material-strength or architecture work.

ontology_before: The graph treated broad construction practice as a direct enabling prerequisite for Galileo's projectile-motion kinematics.
ontology_after: The graph keeps the ballistics and mathematical-method lineage while leaving construction/material-strength practice outside this scoped node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: direct ballistics_and_kinematics_galileo -> construction edge removed.
would_reject_if: A future source shows Galileo's projectile-motion theorem required construction methods rather than artillery practice and mathematical analysis.
The node is later rescoped to include Galileo's strength-of-materials science instead of projectile motion and kinematics.
short_reason: Construction belonged to Galileo's separate materials-strength work, not this projectile-motion node.
